cards.title: Circadian Biology — Clinical Overview
cards._legacyHtml:
Master clock
The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) in the hypothalamus is the master circadian clock, entrained by light. Every organ has a peripheral clock. Misalignment between master and peripheral clocks = chronodisruption = disease.
TimeCircadian-Optimised FunctionClinical implication
6–8 AMCortisol awakening response (CAR) peak, testosterone peak, blood pressure riseBest time for: cognitively demanding tasks, exercise (strength), high-protein meal
10 AM–12 PMPeak alertness, reaction time, muscle coordinationBest time for: consultations, complex procedures, learning
2–4 PMSecond cortisol peak, body temperature peak, peak athletic performanceBest time for: cardiovascular exercise ★, physical therapy
6–8 PM↓ Core body temperature begins, melatonin preparationAvoid heavy meals, bright light, vigorous exercise
9–10 PMMelatonin onset (in dark conditions)Dim lights, blue light blocking, wind-down routine
2–4 AMMelatonin peak, growth hormone surge, liver detoxification Phase ISleep disruption here = impaired GH, cellular repair, detox
🟡 Light as a drug
Morning bright light (10,000 lux, 30 minutes) is the most powerful circadian entraining tool — more effective than any supplement. It advances the circadian phase, reduces depression scores, boosts morning cortisol, and improves sleep onset. Blue light in the evening (phones, LED screens) delays melatonin by 2–3 hours even at low intensities. Blue-light-blocking glasses worn after 7pm restore melatonin timing without screen avoidance.

cards.title: Time-Restricted Eating (TRE) — Evidence
cards._legacyHtml:
16:8 TRE
16h fast / 8h eat window
Optimal eat window
8 AM – 4 PM (early TRE)
Metabolic benefit threshold
≥12 hr fast
Autophagy activation
>14–16 hr fast
TRE ProtocolEvidenceKey outcome
Early TRE (8am–4pm)RCT — Sutton et al. 2018↓ Insulin, ↓ BP, ↑ insulin sensitivity (no weight loss required)
16:8 (12pm–8pm)Multiple RCTs↓ Body weight, ↓ triglycerides, ↓ blood glucose
5:2 fastingMultiple RCTsEqual to continuous calorie restriction for weight, better adherence
Prolonged fasting (3–5 day)Longo — ProLon fasting-mimicking↑ Stem cell regeneration, ↓ IGF-1, immune reset
🟡 Aligning eating with the sun
Insulin sensitivity is highest in the morning and declines throughout the day (clock-gene-driven). The same meal eaten at 8am causes a 30% lower glucose spike than the same meal at 8pm. Front-loading calories (large breakfast, small dinner) — shown in RCT to outperform the reverse for weight, HbA1c, and triglycerides. Simple prescription: "Eat like a king at breakfast, a prince at lunch, a pauper at dinner."

cards.title: Sleep Medicine — Naturopathic Assessment
cards._legacyHtml:
Optimal sleep duration
7–9 hours (adults)
Deep sleep (N3)
15–25% of total
REM sleep
20–25% of total
Sleep latency
<20 minutes (normal)
Sleep disorderAssessment toolNatural interventions
Insomnia (onset)PSQI score, sleep diarySleep restriction therapy ★, stimulus control, passionflower, L-theanine, magnesium glycinate 400mg
Insomnia (maintenance)Actigraphy, PSQICBT-I ★ (most effective), ashwagandha, valerian, 5-HTP 100mg
Circadian phase delayMCTQ (chronotype)Morning light therapy, melatonin 0.5mg at desired bedtime-2hr, avoid evening screens
Sleep apnoea screenSTOP-BANG ≥3Refer for polysomnography; weight loss (10% ↓ weight = 30% ↓ AHI); positional therapy
🟡 CBT-I — first-line for insomnia
Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) is Grade A evidence, superior to sleeping pills in RCTs, with lasting benefit vs drug rebound. Components: sleep restriction, stimulus control, cognitive restructuring, sleep hygiene. Available via Sleepio app (RCT-validated digital CBT-I). Always prescribe before or alongside supplements. Melatonin: only 0.5mg needed for phase-shifting — higher doses don't improve sleep quality, cause grogginess.

cards.title: Lifestyle Medicine — The Six Pillars
cards._legacyHtml:
Lifestyle Medicine — The New Preventive Standard
The American College of Lifestyle Medicine defines 6 pillars: Nutrition, Physical Activity, Restorative Sleep, Stress Management, Avoidance of Risky Substances, Positive Social Connection. These 6 pillars address 80% of chronic disease burden. BNYS training uniquely qualifies practitioners to prescribe all 6.
PillarEvidence SummaryBNYS Prescription
NutritionMediterranean diet reduces CVD 30%, T2DM 25%, cognitive decline 35%. Whole-food plant-based reduces cancer risk 15–40%.Mediterranean or whole-food plant-based. Minimise ultra-processed. 30+ plant foods/week (microbiome diversity).
Physical Activity150 min moderate aerobic + 2× resistance/week reduces all-cause mortality 30–35%Walking prescription (10,000 steps/day), resistance training 2×/week, post-meal walks ★★★
Restorative Sleep7–9 hrs/night. <6 hrs: 2× T2DM risk, 1.5× CV mortality, immune suppression, weight gainCBT-I for insomnia. Sleep hygiene protocol. 10pm–6am window optimal. Melatonin 0.5mg.
Stress ManagementChronic stress = 50% ↑ CV risk, 40% ↑ cancer risk. Mindfulness ★★★ reduces cortisol 25%.MBSR 8-week ★★★, pranayama daily, HRV biofeedback, nature exposure, social connection.
Avoidance of Risky SubstancesSmoking cessation: most impactful single health intervention available (halves CV risk within 1 year)Smoking cessation counselling + NRT. Alcohol <14 units/week. Recreational drug screen.
Positive Social ConnectionSocial isolation = equivalent risk to smoking 15 cigarettes/day (Holt-Lunstad). Strong relationships = 50% improved survival.Prescribe: group yoga/exercise class, volunteering, community engagement, pet ownership, regular family meals.

cards.title: The 4 Lifestyle Pillars — Evidence-Based Framework
cards._legacyHtml:
BNYS 4 Pillars
The 4 pillars of naturopathic lifestyle medicine map to the most evidence-based levers for reversing and preventing chronic disease: Diet, Exercise, Sleep, and Stress Management. Applying all 4 simultaneously creates compounding effects that exceed any single intervention.
PillarMinimum Effective DoseSynergy
DietMediterranean or plant-rich, <10% ultra-processed, 30g fibre/dayReduces systemic inflammation → improves sleep quality + exercise recovery
Exercise150 min moderate aerobic + 2× resistance/week + daily movement breaks↑ BDNF → ↑ sleep quality. ↑ insulin sensitivity → stabilises energy for stress resilience.
Sleep7–9 hours, consistent wake time, sleep efficiency >85%Restores cortisol rhythm → improves stress response. Repairs gut lining → supports dietary goals.
Stress Management20 min mindfulness or pranayama daily + adequate social connection↓ Cortisol → ↓ craving for junk food, ↑ motivation for exercise, ↑ sleep quality.
🟡 The Virtuous Circle
Each pillar reinforces the others — this is why lifestyle medicine programmes consistently outperform single-intervention trials. A patient who starts with even one pillar (e.g., a daily 30-min walk) typically finds that sleep improves, stress reduces, dietary choices improve, and energy for exercise increases. When all 4 pillars are optimised simultaneously: PREDIMED data shows 30% cardiovascular risk reduction; DIRECT trial shows T2DM remission in 46% of patients at 1 year — without any medications.

cards.title: Jet Lag & Shift Work — Circadian Disruption Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
Circadian Disruption — A Modern Epidemic
20% of the working population does shift work. Shift workers have: 40% ↑ cardiovascular disease risk, 50% ↑ T2DM risk, 30% ↑ cancer risk, 2× depression prevalence, and significantly shortened lifespan. Jet lag affects millions of international travellers. Both disrupt the master clock (SCN) and peripheral clocks in every organ.
SituationProtocol
Eastward jet lag (harder — advance phase)Pre-trip: melatonin 0.5mg at destination bedtime 3 days before. During: expose to morning light at destination. Avoid bright light in evenings. Melatonin at destination bedtime for 3–5 nights post-arrival.
Westward jet lag (easier — delay phase)Pre-trip: no advance preparation needed. During: stay awake until destination bedtime. Evening light exposure. Melatonin 0.5mg at destination bedtime for 2–3 nights.
Night shift workersConsistency is critical — same shift pattern whenever possible. Blackout curtains + white noise for daytime sleep. Blue-light blocking glasses BEFORE the shift ends (helps preserve melatonin phase). Exercise at the same time in each shift cycle. Meal timing: eat main meals during waking (not at 3am — prevents metabolic syndrome). Vit D supplementation (shift workers chronically sun-deprived).
Rotating shift workersMost damaging pattern — no circadian adaptation possible. Strategies: strategic napping, melatonin timed to sleep block, high-antioxidant diet (oxidative stress from circadian misalignment), omega-3, Vit D. Advocate for fixed or slowly rotating schedules (clockwise rotation preferable — phase delay is easier than advance).

cards.title: Sleep Architecture — Stages, Functions & Clinical Implications
cards._legacyHtml:
N1 (Light sleep)
5% of sleep · Theta waves · Hypnic jerks · Transition stage
N2 (Core sleep)
45–55% · Sleep spindles + K-complexes · Memory consolidation begins · Body temperature ↓
N3 (Deep sleep / SWS)
15–20% · Delta waves · Growth hormone surge ★ · Immune repair · Hardest to wake · Occurs 1st half of night
REM sleep
20–25% · Dreaming · Emotional processing · Creativity · Memory integration · Occurs 2nd half of night ★
Sleep deprivation effectMechanismNaturopathic intervention
↑ Ghrelin, ↓ leptin → hunger ★Even 1 night of 5h sleep → +500 kcal next-day intakeSleep extension improves weight management
↓ Insulin sensitivity4h sleep × 6 nights = pre-diabetic glucose profilePrioritise 7–9h; magnesium + berberine if T2DM
↑ CRP, IL-6 inflammationSleep <6h → ↑ inflammatory markers within 1 weekConsistent sleep schedule; reduce light at night
↓ Vaccine efficacy5h sleep night before flu vaccine = 50% antibody responseSleep >7h before scheduled vaccinations
↓ Memory consolidationREM processing converts short-term → long-term memoryDon't study past midnight; sleep before exams
Glymphatic clearance failure ★Brain clears amyloid-beta and tau during N3 sleep (2× clearance vs waking)Side-sleeping optimises glymphatic drainage
🟡 The glymphatic system — sleep as brain detox
During deep N3 sleep, astrocytes shrink 60% — creating channels through which CSF flushes amyloid-beta, tau, and metabolic waste from the brain (glymphatic system). 1 poor night of sleep increases CSF amyloid-beta. Chronic poor sleep is one of the strongest modifiable risk factors for Alzheimer's disease. Side-sleeping (lateral position) optimises glymphatic flow by 25% compared to back-sleeping.

cards.title: Social Jetlag & Chronotype
cards._legacyHtml:
Social Jetlag — The Hidden Metabolic Disruptor
Social jetlag is the discrepancy between biological clock timing and social/work schedule. Measured by the difference between sleep midpoint on free days vs work days (typically 1–2 hours in most adults, but up to 5 hours in extreme "night owls"). Social jetlag of 1 hour = 33% higher odds of obesity, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease.
ChronotypeCharacteristicsPrevalenceClinical Implications
Morning type (lark)Natural sleep 10pm–6am. Alert in AM. Afternoon fatigue. Early cortisol peak. Earlier genetic setting of circadian clock (Per3 gene short variant).~25% of populationSocial schedule usually aligned — low social jetlag. Risk: insufficient sleep if evening commitments extend late. Stable metabolic health if schedule maintained.
Intermediate typeNatural sleep 11pm–7am. Most common.~50% of populationModerate social jetlag risk. Standard advice mostly applies.
Evening type (owl)Natural sleep 1–2am → 9–10am. Alert in evening. Peak cognitive performance 8–10pm. Genetic — cannot "will" themselves to be morning people.~25% of populationHigh social jetlag when forced to early schedules. ↑ risk: T2DM, obesity, depression, alcohol/substance use, insomnia. Evening type + early work schedule = chronic "jetlag" every week.
1
Measuring Chronotype — Munich ChronoType Questionnaire (MCTQ) Free online: chronotype.quiz. Key metric: MSFsc (sleep midpoint on free days, sleep-corrected). This is the biological clock "truth" — uninfluenced by alarms or social obligations. Compare to work day sleep midpoint. Difference = social jetlag (in hours). Clinical application: prescribe TRE, meal timing, exercise, and supplement timing based on chronotype — not population averages. Morning type: TRE 8am–4pm. Evening type: 12–8pm (avoid forcing early window — creates physiological conflict).
2
Reducing Social Jetlag — Protocol Keep wake time consistent within 30 min every day (including weekends). Morning light: 10–30 min outdoor bright light within 30 min of waking — strongest zeitgeber. Evening light: blue-light blocking glasses from 8pm (prevents melatonin suppression). Strategic melatonin for phase advancement (owls): 0.5mg at 7pm (5–6 hrs before natural sleep time — shifts clock earlier by 1–2 hrs over 2 weeks). Exercise timing: morning exercise shifts circadian clock earlier (beneficial for owls). Meal timing: consistent meal timing reinforces peripheral clocks.

cards.title: Chronopharmacology — Timing Drugs & Supplements
cards._legacyHtml:
Chronopharmacology — When You Take It Matters As Much As What You Take
Drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and efficacy vary with time of day due to circadian rhythms in: gastric pH and motility, hepatic CYP450 enzyme activity, renal clearance, receptor expression, and target organ function. Optimising timing can significantly improve efficacy and reduce side effects.
Drug/SupplementOptimal TimingReasonEvidence
Statins (simvastatin, fluvastatin)Evening/bedtimeHepatic cholesterol synthesis peaks at night (2–4am). Evening statin = blocks synthesis at peak. Exception: atorvastatin and rosuvastatin (long half-life) — morning or evening equally effective.★★★ Multiple pharmacokinetic studies
Aspirin (cardiovascular)BedtimeMorning platelet aggregation is highest (reason MI peaks 6–10am). Bedtime aspirin: better platelet inhibition at morning peak + modestly lowers BP. HYGIA study: bedtime BP meds vs AM → 45% ↓ CV events.★★★ HYGIA chronotherapy trial
MetforminWith mealsReduces GI side effects. Timing relative to meal more important than time of day. Largest dose with dinner (largest meal in many Indian households).Standard practice
Levothyroxine30–60 min before breakfast on empty stomachFood, coffee, calcium, iron all reduce absorption by 25–40%. Must be taken fasted. Consistent timing critical for stable TSH.★★★ Well established
Iron supplementsMorning, fasted, with Vit CHepcidin (iron absorption regulator) lowest in morning → maximum absorption. Avoid: afternoon/evening (hepcidin peaks after noon — blocks absorption). Alternate day dosing > daily (allows hepcidin reset).★★★ Moretti 2015 — alternate day > daily iron
MagnesiumEvening/bedtimeMuscle relaxant effect beneficial for sleep. Mg supports melatonin production. No absorption advantage to morning timing.Clinical optimisation
Vitamin D3Morning or midday, with largest fat-containing mealFat-soluble — requires dietary fat for micellar absorption. Morning light exposure potentiates D3 activation. Some evidence evening D3 may delay sleep onset.★★ Clinical optimisation
Omega-3With meals, split across 2–3 doses if largeFat-soluble — maximise absorption with food. Splitting reduces fish burp side effects. Morning + evening dosing most practical.Standard practice
Probiotics30 min before meals (or with first bite)Gastric acid lowest just before eating. Bacteria survive better in near-neutral pre-meal stomach vs highly acidic post-meal stomach. Most manufacturers recommend pre-meal.★★ Gastric acid survival studies
B vitaminsMorning (with breakfast)Energy metabolism support. Some B vitamins (B6, B3 especially) can cause mild stimulation — morning timing avoids sleep disruption. Riboflavin turns urine bright yellow — warn patients.Clinical optimisation

cards.title: Seasonal Health — Ayurvedic Ritucharya
cards._legacyHtml:
Ritucharya — Seasonal Lifestyle Regimen
Ritucharya (ritu = season, charya = regimen) is the Ayurvedic system of aligning diet, lifestyle, and daily routine with seasonal changes. The 6 seasons (Ritu) correspond to Indian climate cycles. Following Ritucharya maintains doshic balance, prevents seasonal diseases, and optimises vitality. This is fundamentally a circannual chronobiology — consistent with modern research on seasonal variation in immune function, metabolism, and mood.
Ritu (Season)Months (approx)Dominant DoshaDietary GuidanceLifestyle Guidance
Shishira (Late winter)Jan–FebKapha accumulation, Vata residualWarm, heavy, nourishing: sesame, wheat, sugarcane, ghee, milk, root vegetables. Avoid: cold, light, dry foods.Warm oil massage (abhyanga). Heavy blankets. Warm baths. Reduce cold exposure. Protect Agni (digestive fire).
Vasanta (Spring)Mar–AprKapha provocation and liquefactionLight, dry, digestive-enhancing: bitter vegetables, honey, barley, old wheat, light soups. Avoid: heavy, sweet, oily, sour foods. Reduce dairy. Ideal for cleansing (Vamana traditionally).Exercise vigorous (body strong, agni weakening kapha). Dry massage (udvartana). Avoid day sleep. Seasonal cleanse ideal. Neti pot daily.
Grishma (Summer)May–JunVata increase, Pitta provocationCool, sweet, light, hydrating: coconut water, buttermilk, rice, cucumber, pomegranate, mint. Avoid: pungent, salty, sour, hot food. Reduce alcohol, exercise intensity.Cool environment. Light cotton clothing. Rest in heat of day. Cool water baths. Coconut oil for skin and scalp. Avoid excessive sun exposure between 10am–4pm.
Varsha (Monsoon)Jul–AugVata provocation + Pitta accumulationWarm, light, easily digestible. Old rice, lentils, ghee, rock salt. Boil all drinking water (monsoon pathogens). Avoid: raw/cold foods, heavy meats, leftover food. Fermented foods beneficial (probiotic-rich environment).Avoid getting wet. Warm clothing. Light exercise. Digestive support (ginger tea, trikatu). Panchakarma ideal season (virechana for pitta). Mosquito prevention (dengue/malaria peak).
Sharad (Autumn)Sep–OctPitta provocation (accumulated summer heat releases)Sweet, bitter, astringent tastes. Cooling foods: ghee, rice, wheat, milk, pomegranate. Avoid: pungent, sour, salty, oily. Avoid fasting (pitta already high — starvation increases it).Moonlight exposure (cooling — Chandra sevan). Vamana/virechana for Pitta disorders. Cooling pranayama (Sheetali, Sitkari). Avoid midday sun. Eye care important (pitta governs vision).
Hemanta (Early winter)Nov–DecVata pacified, Kapha beginningNourishing, heavy, warming: sesame, urad dal, sugarcane, meat (for non-vegetarians), milk, ghee, almonds. Good digestive fire — can eat more. Warm soups, stews.Exercise strongest (body strength peak). Oil massage. Warm baths. Protect from cold and wind. Ideal for building ojas (vitality). Good season for new health programmes.
🟡 Modern Seasonal Health Patterns
Modern research validates seasonal health variation: Vit D levels 30% lower in winter (India: even in tropical areas due to clothing + indoor lifestyle). NK cell activity and immunity lower in winter months. Seasonal affective disorder (SAD) and depression higher in winter. Viral respiratory infections peak in monsoon and winter. Serotonin varies seasonally. Ritucharya intuitively captured these patterns. Clinical application: adjust naturopathic supplement prescriptions seasonally (higher Vit D + Vit C in monsoon/winter, lighter diet + digestive support in summer). Preventive seasonal cleansing (before Vasanta and Varsha) is a powerful health maintenance strategy.

↓ Insulin sensitivity4h sleep × 6 nights = pre-diabetic glucose profilePrioritise 7–9h; magnesium + berberine if T2DM
↑ CRP, IL-6 inflammationSleep <6h → ↑ inflammatory markers within 1 weekConsistent sleep schedule; reduce light at night
↓ Vaccine efficacy5h sleep night before flu vaccine = 50% antibody responseSleep >7h before scheduled vaccinations
↓ Memory consolidationREM processing converts short-term → long-term memoryDon't study past midnight; sleep before exams
Glymphatic clearance failure ★Brain clears amyloid-beta and tau during N3 sleep (2× clearance vs waking)Side-sleeping optimises glymphatic drainage
🟡 The glymphatic system — sleep as brain detox
During deep N3 sleep, astrocytes shrink 60% — creating channels through which CSF flushes amyloid-beta, tau, and metabolic waste from the brain (glymphatic system). 1 poor night of sleep increases CSF amyloid-beta. Chronic poor sleep is one of the strongest modifiable risk factors for Alzheimer's disease. Side-sleeping (lateral position) optimises glymphatic flow by 25% compared to back-sleeping.

cards.title: Chronopharmacology — Timing Drugs & Supplements
cards._legacyHtml:
Chronopharmacology — When You Take It Matters As Much As What You Take
Drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and efficacy vary with time of day due to circadian rhythms in: gastric pH and motility, hepatic CYP450 enzyme activity, renal clearance, receptor expression, and target organ function. Optimising timing can significantly improve efficacy and reduce side effects.
Drug/SupplementOptimal TimingReasonEvidence
Statins (simvastatin, fluvastatin)Evening/bedtimeHepatic cholesterol synthesis peaks at night (2–4am). Evening statin = blocks synthesis at peak. Exception: atorvastatin and rosuvastatin (long half-life) — morning or evening equally effective.★★★ Multiple pharmacokinetic studies
Aspirin (cardiovascular)BedtimeMorning platelet aggregation is highest (reason MI peaks 6–10am). Bedtime aspirin: better platelet inhibition at morning peak + modestly lowers BP. HYGIA study: bedtime BP meds vs AM → 45% ↓ CV events.★★★ HYGIA chronotherapy trial
MetforminWith mealsReduces GI side effects. Timing relative to meal more important than time of day. Largest dose with dinner (largest meal in many Indian households).Standard practice
Levothyroxine30–60 min before breakfast on empty stomachFood, coffee, calcium, iron all reduce absorption by 25–40%. Must be taken fasted. Consistent timing critical for stable TSH.★★★ Well established
Iron supplementsMorning, fasted, with Vit CHepcidin (iron absorption regulator) lowest in morning → maximum absorption. Avoid: afternoon/evening (hepcidin peaks after noon — blocks absorption). Alternate day dosing > daily (allows hepcidin reset).★★★ Moretti 2015 — alternate day > daily iron
MagnesiumEvening/bedtimeMuscle relaxant effect beneficial for sleep. Mg supports melatonin production. No absorption advantage to morning timing.Clinical optimisation
Vitamin D3Morning or midday, with largest fat-containing mealFat-soluble — requires dietary fat for micellar absorption. Morning light exposure potentiates D3 activation. Some evidence evening D3 may delay sleep onset.★★ Clinical optimisation
Omega-3With meals, split across 2–3 doses if largeFat-soluble — maximise absorption with food. Splitting reduces fish burp side effects. Morning + evening dosing most practical.Standard practice
Probiotics30 min before meals (or with first bite)Gastric acid lowest just before eating. Bacteria survive better in near-neutral pre-meal stomach vs highly acidic post-meal stomach. Most manufacturers recommend pre-meal.★★ Gastric acid survival studies
B vitaminsMorning (with breakfast)Energy metabolism support. Some B vitamins (B6, B3 especially) can cause mild stimulation — morning timing avoids sleep disruption. Riboflavin turns urine bright yellow — warn patients.Clinical optimisation

cards.title: Seasonal Health — Ayurvedic Ritucharya
cards._legacyHtml:
Ritucharya — Seasonal Lifestyle Regimen
Ritucharya (ritu = season, charya = regimen) is the Ayurvedic system of aligning diet, lifestyle, and daily routine with seasonal changes. The 6 seasons (Ritu) correspond to Indian climate cycles. Following Ritucharya maintains doshic balance, prevents seasonal diseases, and optimises vitality. This is fundamentally a circannual chronobiology — consistent with modern research on seasonal variation in immune function, metabolism, and mood.
Ritu (Season)Months (approx)Dominant DoshaDietary GuidanceLifestyle Guidance
Shishira (Late winter)Jan–FebKapha accumulation, Vata residualWarm, heavy, nourishing: sesame, wheat, sugarcane, ghee, milk, root vegetables. Avoid: cold, light, dry foods.Warm oil massage (abhyanga). Heavy blankets. Warm baths. Reduce cold exposure. Protect Agni (digestive fire).
Vasanta (Spring)Mar–AprKapha provocation and liquefactionLight, dry, digestive-enhancing: bitter vegetables, honey, barley, old wheat, light soups. Avoid: heavy, sweet, oily, sour foods. Reduce dairy. Ideal for cleansing (Vamana traditionally).Exercise vigorous (body strong, agni weakening kapha). Dry massage (udvartana). Avoid day sleep. Seasonal cleanse ideal. Neti pot daily.
Grishma (Summer)May–JunVata increase, Pitta provocationCool, sweet, light, hydrating: coconut water, buttermilk, rice, cucumber, pomegranate, mint. Avoid: pungent, salty, sour, hot food. Reduce alcohol, exercise intensity.Cool environment. Light cotton clothing. Rest in heat of day. Cool water baths. Coconut oil for skin and scalp. Avoid excessive sun exposure between 10am–4pm.
Varsha (Monsoon)Jul–AugVata provocation + Pitta accumulationWarm, light, easily digestible. Old rice, lentils, ghee, rock salt. Boil all drinking water (monsoon pathogens). Avoid: raw/cold foods, heavy meats, leftover food. Fermented foods beneficial (probiotic-rich environment).Avoid getting wet. Warm clothing. Light exercise. Digestive support (ginger tea, trikatu). Panchakarma ideal season (virechana for pitta). Mosquito prevention (dengue/malaria peak).
Sharad (Autumn)Sep–OctPitta provocation (accumulated summer heat releases)Sweet, bitter, astringent tastes. Cooling foods: ghee, rice, wheat, milk, pomegranate. Avoid: pungent, sour, salty, oily. Avoid fasting (pitta already high — starvation increases it).Moonlight exposure (cooling — Chandra sevan). Vamana/virechana for Pitta disorders. Cooling pranayama (Sheetali, Sitkari). Avoid midday sun. Eye care important (pitta governs vision).
Hemanta (Early winter)Nov–DecVata pacified, Kapha beginningNourishing, heavy, warming: sesame, urad dal, sugarcane, meat (for non-vegetarians), milk, ghee, almonds. Good digestive fire — can eat more. Warm soups, stews.Exercise strongest (body strength peak). Oil massage. Warm baths. Protect from cold and wind. Ideal for building ojas (vitality). Good season for new health programmes.
🟡 Modern Seasonal Health Patterns
Modern research validates seasonal health variation: Vit D levels 30% lower in winter (India: even in tropical areas due to clothing + indoor lifestyle). NK cell activity and immunity lower in winter months. Seasonal affective disorder (SAD) and depression higher in winter. Viral respiratory infections peak in monsoon and winter. Serotonin varies seasonally. Ritucharya intuitively captured these patterns. Clinical application: adjust naturopathic supplement prescriptions seasonally (higher Vit D + Vit C in monsoon/winter, lighter diet + digestive support in summer). Preventive seasonal cleansing (before Vasanta and Varsha) is a powerful health maintenance strategy.
